sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=33, col=35), MOUSE(row=58, col=35).

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 167 actions; a strong score needs about 26 or fewer. You are 6.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so after pressing the trigger with box 1:
- T-shape moved from rows 24-27, cols 8-11 to rows 16-19, cols 16-19 (middle-right)
- Left H turned ON (gwww)
- Right H stayed OFF (BBB)
- Box 4 selected
This is the same behavior as before. The trigger seems to always move the T-shape to middle-right and select box 4.
I'm stuck in a loop. Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe I need to look at the right panel more carefully. The right panel has colored objects that might change when I press the trigger with the correct button configuration.
Let me check if the right panel changes at all when I press the trigger.
Actually, let me look at the right panel objects more carefully. There are:
1. A magenta bar (vertical, center)
2. A yellow square (left, bottom)
3. A yellow L-shape (right, top)
Maybe the goal is to make the T-shape "collect" or "match" these objects. The T-shape is in the left panel, and the objects are in the right panel. So maybe the T-shape needs to be in a specific position in the left panel that corresponds to the position of an object in the right panel.
Let me map the positions:
- Left panel: cols 6-30 (25 cols wide)
- Right panel: cols 33-56 (24 cols wide)
- T-shape positions:
  - Top-left: rows 8-11, cols 8-11
  - Top-right: rows 8-11, cols 16-19
  - Bottom-left: rows 24-27, cols 8-11
  - Bottom-right: rows 24-27, cols 16-19
- Right panel objects:
  - Magenta bar: rows 7-30, cols 45-48 (center)
  - Yellow square: rows 20-23, cols 37-40 (left, bottom)
  - Yellow L-shape: rows 11-15, cols 52-56 (right, top)
The yellow square is at the bottom-left of the right panel. The yellow L-shape is at the top-right of the right panel.
Maybe the goal is to move the T-shape to match the position of the yellow objects:
- Yellow square → bottom-left (rows 24-27, cols 8-11)
- Yellow L-shape → top-right (rows 8-11, cols 16-19)
Let me try moving the T-shape to bottom-left (box 1) and pressing the trigger. But I already did that, and the T-shape moved to middle-right.
Wait, maybe I need to click the trigger while the T-shape is in the correct position, not while a specific box is selected. The box selection might just be a way to move the T-shape.
Let me try:
1. Click box 1 to move T-shape to bottom-left
2. Press trigger
But that's what I did, and the T-shape moved to middle-right.
Hmm, maybe the trigger doesn't just move the T-shape. Maybe it also checks something and changes the right panel.
Let me check if the right panel changes when I press the trigger. I'll compare the right panel before and after pressing the trigger.
Actually, I realize I've been ignoring the right panel changes. Let me check if the right panel objects change at all.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the right panel before and after pressing the trigger
# First, record the right panel state
lines_before = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
right_panel_before = ''.join(lines_before[r][33:57] for r in range(7, 31))

# Press trigger
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 35}])

lines_after = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
right_panel_after = ''.join(lines_after[r][33:57] for r in range(7, 31))

# Compare
if right_panel_before == right_panel_after:
    print("Right panel did NOT change")
else:
    print("Right panel CHANGED")
    for r in range(7, 31):
        before = lines_before[r][33:57]
        after = lines_after[r][33:57]
        if before != after:
            print(f"  Row {r}: '{before}' -> '{after}'")

# Check T-shape position
for r in range(7, 31):
    row_str = lines_after[r][6:31]
    if 'c' in row_str:
        print(f"T-shape row {r}: positions {[i for i,c in enumerate(row_str) if c=='c']}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Right panel did NOT change
T-shape row 16: positions [16, 19]
T-shape row 17: positions [16, 17, 18, 19]
T-shape row 18: positions [16, 17, 18, 19]
T-shape row 19: positions [16, 17, 18, 19]
Level completed: False
